---
title: "Example: Evaluating Fits"
filters:
  - shinylive
---

::: {.callout-note}
This page shows an example of a hypothetical lecture note that combines several features 
of Quarto. This was inspired by notes written by Magnus Kjærgaard.
:::

Computers are very good at least-squares optimization. They can often find
parameters for a model even when the model is not a sensible description of the
experiment. The scientific question is therefore not only whether a curve can be
drawn through the data, but whether the fit is good enough, simple enough, and
consistent with what we know about the system.

In kinetic experiments, a fitted parameter might be interpreted as a rate
constant. That interpretation only makes sense if the model is appropriate for
the data. A high $R^2$ value is useful, but it is not a substitute for looking at
the data, the residuals, and the model assumptions.


```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-diagram
%%| fig-cap: A practical workflow for evaluating a fitted model. Using a mermaid [diagram](../diagrams.qmd)
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart TD
  data["Data"]
  model["Candidate model"]
  fit["Fit parameters"]
  residuals["Residuals"]
  parsimony["Parsimony"]
  theory["Theory check"]
  decision["Accept or revise"]

  data --> model --> fit --> residuals --> parsimony --> theory --> decision
  residuals -. "pattern?" .-> model
  theory -. "implausible?" .-> model
```

## Experimental variation

Measurements from biochemical experiments are not expected to lie exactly on a
theoretical curve. Technical variation can come from pipetting, temperature
fluctuations, instrument precision, and other small differences between repeated
measurements. For small data sets, showing the individual measurements is often
more informative than only showing error bars.

@fig-synthetic-data creates a small synthetic decay experiment with technical
replicates. The mean values and standard deviations summarize the replicates, but
the individual points are still visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-synthetic-data
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Synthetic kinetic data shown as individual technical replicates and mean ± SD. Using [Python code cells](../../code_interactive/python-code-cells.qmd)"
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
x = np.linspace(0, 20, 11)
true_y = 1.05 * np.exp(-0.11 * x) + 0.04
replicates = true_y[:, None] + rng.normal(0, 0.045, size=(len(x), 4))
means = replicates.mean(axis=1)
sd = replicates.std(axis=1, ddof=1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
for column in replicates.T:
    ax.scatter(x, column, color="#5b7f95", alpha=0.55, s=28)
ax.errorbar(
    x,
    means,
    yerr=sd,
    fmt="o",
    color="#202020",
    ecolor="#202020",
    capsize=3,
    label="mean ± SD",
)
ax.plot(x, true_y, color="#b84a39", label="underlying curve")
ax.set(xlabel="Time", ylabel="Signal")
ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-note title="What the error bars can and cannot tell us"}
If deviations between data and model are similar in size to the experimental
variation, the model may be adequate within error. If deviations are much larger,
or if they follow a systematic pattern, the model needs more scrutiny.
:::

## Residual explorer

Residuals are the vertical differences between the observed data and the fitted
model. For an appropriate model, residuals should usually scatter around zero
without a clear pattern. A poor model can still have a convincing-looking curve
and a high $R^2$, but the residuals often reveal what is wrong.

Use the widget to simulate different kinds of data and fit different models.
Try fitting a line to exponential data, then inspect the residual plot. Then
compare the single exponential, double exponential, and quadratic polynomial
when extrapolation is enabled.

::: {#fig-residual-explorer .column-page-inset-right}
```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 760

{{< include shiny_app.py >}}
```

Interactive residual explorer using [Shiny Apps](../shiny.qmd). The widget shows
the fitted curve, residuals, and metrics, but it deliberately does not decide
whether the model is acceptable. That judgment should come from comparing the
visual pattern, the parameter count, and the scientific context.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Do not stop at R²"}
The linear model can achieve a respectable $R^2$ on exponential data, especially
when the noise level is high or the measured range is short. The residual plot is
often more diagnostic because it shows whether deviations are random or
systematic.
:::

## Parsimony and theoretical soundness

More parameters usually make it easier to reduce the residual sum of squares.
That does not automatically make the model better. A double exponential has more
freedom than a single exponential, and a polynomial can mimic many shapes inside
the measured range. Parsimony asks us to prefer the simplest model that explains
the data adequately.

Theoretical soundness is a separate check. For a kinetic decay experiment, an
exponential model may be justified by the reaction mechanism. A polynomial might
fit the measured points just as well, but it can predict an unphysical increase
when extrapolated beyond the data. Wrong models often give wrong predictions
outside the region where they were fitted.

## Authoring note

This page uses ShinyLive because the learner interaction changes the data and
refits the selected model in Python. A Plotly or Observable JS widget would be a
good fit for exploring prepared results, but this example needs live
recalculation with `scipy.optimize.curve_fit`.

The code is folded rather than hidden. That keeps the page readable as course
material while still allowing handbook readers to inspect how the synthetic data,
fits, residuals, and embedded app are built.
